name: Mzansi Mix Vol. 1
description: South African general knowledge trivia — culture, history, geography, food, slang, music, and more!
author: Game Night Crew

questions.question: A R5 coin is round and flat. How many sides does it have?
questions.explanation: Eish, this one's easy! Any flat coin has 2 sides — heads and tails. Don't let the '5' on the coin confuse you!

questions.question: What is the capital city of South Africa that houses the Union Buildings?
questions.explanation: Pretoria is the administrative capital and home to the Union Buildings, where the President's offices are. SA is unique with three capital cities — Cape Town (legislative) and Bloemfontein (judicial) are the others. Lekker fact!

questions.question: If today is Monday in Joburg, what day is it in Cape Town?
questions.explanation: Both cities are in the same country and time zone! It's still Monday in Cape Town. No tricks here, just now you'll overthink it.

questions.question: What popular dried meat snack is South Africa famous for?
questions.explanation: Biltong is SA's pride and joy! It's cured, dried meat seasoned with spices like coriander and vinegar. Don't ever call it jerky around a South African — you'll start a fight, shame!

questions.question: Which is heavier: 1 kg of boerewors or 1 kg of droewors?
questions.explanation: They both weigh 1 kg! A kilogram is a kilogram whether it's boerewors or droewors. Don't let your braai instincts fool you.

questions.question: What does the South African slang word 'lekker' mean?
questions.explanation: Lekker means nice, great, or delicious! It's one of the most-used words in SA. 'That braai was lekker' means it was great. You'll hear it 50 times a day in Mzansi!

questions.question: Which ocean borders the east coast of South Africa?
questions.explanation: The Indian Ocean is on the east coast (Durban side), while the Atlantic Ocean is on the west coast (Cape Town side). That's why Durban's beaches are warmer — the Mozambique Current brings warm water down from the tropics!

questions.question: What is the Afrikaans word for a barbecue in South Africa?
questions.explanation: Braai is the Afrikaans word! Shisa nyama is the isiZulu term for grilled meat, and it's also widely used. But braai is THE word that unites all South Africans. Heritage Day is even called National Braai Day!

questions.question: How many official languages does South Africa have?
questions.explanation: South Africa has 11 official languages! Including English, Afrikaans, isiZulu, isiXhosa, Sesotho, Setswana, Sepedi, Xitsonga, siSwati, Tshivenda, and isiNdebele. That's the most of any country in Africa. Multilingual is the Mzansi way!

questions.question: You have three Woolworths bags. The first has apples, the second has oranges, the third has apples and oranges. ALL labels are wrong. You pick one fruit from the bag labelled 'Apples and Oranges' and it's an apple. What's actually in the bag labelled 'Oranges'?
questions.explanation: Since ALL labels are wrong: The bag labelled 'Apples and Oranges' can't have both — you pulled an apple, so it has only apples. The bag labelled 'Oranges' can't have oranges, and the apples-only bag is taken, so it must have apples and oranges. The bag labelled 'Apples' must have oranges. Lekker logic puzzle!

questions.question: What is the nickname for Johannesburg?
questions.explanation: Joburg is called the City of Gold because of the Witwatersrand gold rush that started in 1886. It's also called Jozi, Egoli (isiZulu for 'place of gold'), and eGoli. The Mother City is Cape Town, by the way!

questions.question: Which South African dish is a hollowed-out loaf of bread filled with curry?
questions.explanation: Bunny chow originated in Durban's Indian community. It's a half or quarter loaf of white bread, hollowed out and filled with curry. The name has nothing to do with rabbits — it likely comes from the Bania merchant caste. It's a Durban icon!

questions.question: Nelson Mandela was imprisoned on which island?
questions.explanation: Robben Island, off the coast of Cape Town, is where Madiba spent 18 of his 27 years in prison. Today it's a UNESCO World Heritage Site and museum. A visit there will give you goosebumps, guaranteed!

questions.question: What does 'eish' express in South African slang?
questions.explanation: Eish is one of the most versatile words in SA! It can express shock, frustration, sympathy, or disbelief depending on how you say it. Load shedding again? Eish! Your team lost? Eish! It's pure Mzansi emotion in one word.

questions.question: Which province is the smallest by area in South Africa?
questions.explanation: Gauteng is the smallest province but the most populated and the economic powerhouse of SA! It's only about 18,178 km squared but home to Joburg and Pretoria. Small but mighty, just like a chilli on a braai plate!

questions.question: What is the name of the famous steam train that runs between Cape Town and Pretoria?
questions.explanation: The Blue Train is one of the most luxurious train journeys in the world! It runs between Pretoria and Cape Town, covering about 1,600 km. Rovos Rail is also a luxury SA train, but the Blue Train is the more famous one. All aboard!

questions.question: Which South African rugby team is known as the Springboks?
options: The provincial team from Western Cape
questions.explanation: The Springboks (Bokke) are the SA national rugby team! They've won the Rugby World Cup four times (1995, 2007, 2019, 2023). The 1995 win is legendary — Madiba wearing the Bok jersey united the nation. Bokke!

questions.question: What is a 'potjiekos'?
options: A stew cooked in a cast-iron pot over coals
questions.explanation: Potjiekos (small pot food) is a traditional SA stew slow-cooked in a three-legged cast-iron pot over coals. The golden rule? Never stir it — you layer the ingredients and let the magic happen. It takes hours but it's worth every minute!

questions.question: In which year did South Africa host the FIFA World Cup?
questions.explanation: SA hosted the 2010 FIFA World Cup — the first ever on African soil! Remember the vuvuzelas? The whole world couldn't stop hearing them. Spain won the tournament, but Mzansi won everyone's hearts. Ke nako!

questions.question: What is Table Mountain's flat top made of?
questions.explanation: Table Mountain is made of sandstone, specifically the Table Mountain Sandstone formation that's about 600 million years old! The flat top was formed by millions of years of erosion. It's one of the oldest mountains in the world. Cape Town's backyard is ancient!

questions.question: What animal is on the South African coat of arms?
questions.explanation: The secretary bird features prominently on SA's coat of arms, representing protection and vigilance. Its uplifted wings symbolise the rise of the nation. The springbok is the rugby emblem, the blue crane is the national bird, but the coat of arms belongs to the secretary bird!

questions.question: Which city is known as the 'Mother City' of South Africa?
questions.explanation: Cape Town is the Mother City because it was the first European settlement in SA, founded by Jan van Riebeeck in 1652. It's where it all began for colonial South Africa. Plus the mountain, the wine, the beaches... what a city!

questions.question: What does 'now now' mean in South African English?
questions.explanation: In SA, 'now now' means soon but not right away. 'Now' means sometime today, 'now now' means soonish, and 'just now' could mean anything from 5 minutes to never. It confuses every tourist, shame!

questions.question: Which South African city is famous for its annual sardine run?
questions.explanation: The sardine run along the KwaZulu-Natal coast near Durban is one of the greatest marine spectacles on Earth! Billions of sardines migrate up the coast between May and July, attracting sharks, dolphins, and birds. It's nature's biggest buffet!

questions.question: What is a 'Gatsby' in Cape Town?
options: A massive sandwich with chips and fillings
questions.explanation: A Gatsby is a Cape Town legend — a long roll stuffed with chips (hot chips!), and your choice of filling like polony, steak, masala steak, or calamari. One Gatsby can feed 2-4 people. It was invented in the Cape Flats and it's a proper working-class hero of a meal!

questions.question: Five Joburg office workers — Ayanda, Bongani, Cynthia, Dumisani, and Fikile — sit in a row. Ayanda sits next to Bongani. Cynthia does NOT sit next to Dumisani. Fikile sits at one end. Bongani sits in the exact middle. Who sits at the other end (not Fikile's end)?
options: Could be either Cynthia or Dumisani
questions.explanation: Bongani is in the middle (position 3). Ayanda sits next to Bongani, so Ayanda is in position 2 or 4. Fikile is at one end (position 1 or 5). Cynthia and Dumisani fill the remaining spots. Since Cynthia can NOT sit next to Dumisani, and since one of them is next to Bongani (pos 2 or 4) and the other at the far end: if Ayanda is in pos 2, Fikile at pos 1, then pos 4 and 5 are left for C and D — but they'd be adjacent (not allowed). So Fikile must be at pos 5, Ayanda at pos 4, leaving pos 1 and 2 for C and D. Cynthia can't be next to Dumisani: pos 1 and 2 are adjacent — contradiction! Try Ayanda at pos 4, Fikile at pos 1: pos 2 and 5 for C and D. They're not adjacent. Cynthia at pos 2 (next to Bongani), Dumisani at pos 5. The other end is Dumisani!

questions.question: Which Big Five animal is NOT actually one of the Big Five?
questions.explanation: The Big Five are lion, leopard, rhino, elephant, and buffalo. The cheetah is NOT one of them! The Big Five were originally named by big-game hunters as the most dangerous animals to hunt on foot. The cheetah, while fast and beautiful, didn't make the list!

questions.question: What is the name of the traditional South African fermented maize porridge drink?
questions.explanation: Mageu (or mahewu) is a traditional non-alcoholic fermented maize drink. It's creamy, slightly sour, and super refreshing. Umqombothi is traditional beer, not porridge-based. Mageu is the working person's energy drink — cheap, filling, and proudly SA!

questions.question: Which famous pass connects the Garden Route town of Knysna to the interior?
questions.explanation: Prince Alfred's Pass is the famous gravel road connecting Knysna to the Langkloof valley. It was built in 1867 and named after Queen Victoria's son. Sani Pass is in KZN/Lesotho, Bainskloof is in the Western Cape near Wellington, and Franschhoek Pass leads to the winelands!

questions.question: What does the Zulu phrase 'Sawubona' mean?
questions.explanation: Sawubona literally means 'I see you' and is used as a greeting in isiZulu. It's deeper than just 'hello' — it acknowledges the other person's existence and dignity. The response is 'Ngikhona' meaning 'I am here.' Beautiful, neh?

questions.question: Which South African musician was known as 'MaBrrr' and the 'Queen of African Pop'?
questions.explanation: Brenda Fassie — MaBrrr herself — was the undisputed Queen of African Pop! Her hit 'Weekend Special' in 1983 made her a continental star. She was bold, controversial, and wildly talented. Miriam Makeba is 'Mama Africa' and Yvonne Chaka Chaka is the 'Princess of Africa.' SA music royalty, all of them!

questions.question: What is the Cradle of Humankind famous for?
questions.explanation: The Cradle of Humankind, a UNESCO World Heritage Site near Joburg, contains the richest concentration of human ancestor fossils in the world! The famous 'Mrs Ples' skull and 'Little Foot' skeleton were found here. We're literally ALL from Mzansi if you go back far enough!

questions.question: Which country does NOT share a border with South Africa?
questions.explanation: Zambia does NOT border South Africa! SA shares borders with Namibia, Botswana, Zimbabwe, Mozambique, Eswatini, and Lesotho (which is completely surrounded by SA). Zambia is further north, separated by Zimbabwe and Botswana. Geography matters!

questions.question: What is the traditional Xhosa ceremony called where young men undergo initiation into manhood?
questions.explanation: Ulwaluko is the Xhosa male initiation ceremony, a sacred rite of passage. Young men go to the bush and return as men (amakrwala). Umhlanga is the Swazi/Zulu Reed Dance, Umemulo is a Zulu coming-of-age for women, and Lobola is bride price. Deep cultural traditions!

questions.question: What is the famous wine region near Cape Town called?
questions.explanation: The Cape Winelands — Stellenbosch, Franschhoek, and Paarl — make up one of the world's top wine-producing regions. SA is the 8th largest wine producer globally. Stellenbosch alone has over 200 wine farms. Time for a wine tasting, neh?

questions.question: Which South African activist and cleric won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1984?
questions.explanation: Archbishop Desmond Tutu won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1984 for his nonviolent campaign against apartheid. Mandela and de Klerk shared it in 1993, and Albert Luthuli won it in 1960. SA has produced four Nobel Peace Prize winners. The Arch was a true legend!

questions.question: What unique feature do the Cango Caves near Oudtshoorn have?
questions.explanation: The Cango Caves are famous for their spectacular dripstone (stalactite and stalagmite) formations, some of which are over 150,000 years old! The caves stretch for 4 km underground near Oudtshoorn in the Western Cape. Cleopatra's Needle is the most famous formation. Underground Mzansi magic!

questions.question: What is a 'kota' (also spelled 'kota') in SA township food culture?
options: A quarter loaf of bread with fillings like chips, polony, and atchar
questions.explanation: A kota is a quarter loaf of bread stuffed with chips, polony, cheese, atchar, and whatever else you fancy! It originated in Soweto and is THE street food of the townships. Cheap, filling, and absolutely delicious. The fancier versions are called 'spihatla' — loaded to the max!

questions.question: What is rooibos?
options: A red bush tea plant unique to South Africa
questions.explanation: Rooibos (meaning 'red bush' in Afrikaans) is a tea plant that grows ONLY in the Cederberg region of the Western Cape. It's caffeine-free, packed with antioxidants, and loved worldwide. South Africa is the only place on Earth it grows naturally. Proudly SA!

questions.question: You have 12 identical-looking Krugerrands, but one is fake and slightly lighter. Using a balance scale, what is the MINIMUM number of weighings guaranteed to find the fake coin?
questions.explanation: Split into 3 groups of 4. Weigh group 1 vs group 2. If they balance, the fake is in group 3; if not, it's in the lighter group. Now you have 4 suspects. Split into groups of 1, 1, and 2. Weigh the two singles — if they balance, weigh the remaining 2 to find the fake. 3 weighings guaranteed. This is a classic logic puzzle, just with Krugerrands!

questions.question: What was District Six in Cape Town?
options: A vibrant multicultural neighbourhood destroyed under apartheid forced removals
questions.explanation: District Six was a vibrant, multicultural inner-city neighbourhood in Cape Town. In 1966, the apartheid government declared it a white area and forcibly removed over 60,000 residents. The District Six Museum keeps the memory alive. One of apartheid's cruelest acts.

questions.question: What is the southernmost point of the African continent?
questions.explanation: Cape Agulhas is the true southernmost point of Africa, NOT the Cape of Good Hope (a very common mistake!). Cape Agulhas is where the Indian and Atlantic Oceans officially meet. The Cape of Good Hope is the most south-western point. Now you know the real answer!

questions.question: What was the original name of the currency used in South Africa before the Rand?
questions.explanation: Before 1961, South Africa used the South African Pound (and before that, the British Pound). The Rand was introduced on 14 February 1961 when SA became a republic. The name 'Rand' comes from the Witwatersrand, the ridge where Joburg's gold was discovered!

questions.question: Which South African Nobel Prize winner discovered that peptic ulcers can be linked to specific bacteria?
options: None — that was an Australian discovery
questions.explanation: Trick question! The peptic ulcer/bacteria discovery was by Australians Barry Marshall and Robin Warren. SA's Nobel science winners are Sydney Brenner (Medicine, 2002, molecular biology), Max Theiler (Medicine, 1951, yellow fever vaccine), and Allan Cormack (Physics, 1979, CT scan). Know your SA science heroes!

questions.question: Which South African-born entrepreneur co-founded PayPal and later founded SpaceX and Tesla?
questions.explanation: Elon Musk was born in Pretoria in 1971! He moved to Canada at 17. Mark Shuttleworth is also a famous SA-born tech entrepreneur — he was the first African in space. SA produces some next-level talent, neh?

questions.question: Three friends buy a R300 braai pack and each pay R100. The butcher realises it should only cost R250 and gives R50 back to a friend to split. The friend keeps R20 and gives R10 back to each of the other two. Now each person paid R90 (2 people) and R80 (the sneaky friend). That's R90 + R90 + R80 = R260. The butcher has R250. That's R510 total but they only started with R300. Where's the extra R210?
options: There is no missing money — the question adds numbers that shouldn't be added together
questions.explanation: This is a classic misdirection puzzle! The R260 paid (R90+R90+R80) ALREADY INCLUDES the R20 the friend kept. The correct accounting: R250 (butcher) + R20 (sneaky friend) + R30 (returned to the three friends) = R300. You shouldn't add the R260 paid to the R250 the butcher has — the R250 is part of the R260! Eish, that's sneaky maths!

questions.question: What year did apartheid officially end with the first democratic elections in South Africa?
questions.explanation: The first democratic elections were held on 27 April 1994, now celebrated as Freedom Day! Nelson Mandela became the first Black president. 1990 was when Mandela was released and the ANC unbanned, but the actual elections were in 1994. A date every South African should know!

questions.question: You're at a 4-way intersection in Bloemfontein. A sign says: 'Turn left to go to the hospital. The library is opposite the hospital. The school is to the right of the library (from the library's perspective, facing the intersection).' You're facing north. Where is the school?
questions.explanation: You face north. Left = west, so hospital is west. Library is opposite hospital = east. From the library facing the intersection (facing west), the school is to the library's right = north. This requires carefully tracking perspectives and directions. Only about 5% would get this right!

questions.question: Nkosi says: 'Two days ago, I was 25. Next year, I'll turn 28.' On what date is Nkosi's birthday and what date is he saying this?
questions.explanation: Nkosi is speaking on January 1st. His birthday is December 31st. Two days ago was December 30th — he was still 25. Yesterday (Dec 31) was his birthday and he turned 26. Today (Jan 1) he is 26. This calendar year (on Dec 31) he'll turn 27. Next year (the following Dec 31) he'll turn 28. It all works because the year boundary falls between his birthday and the day he's speaking! Eish, that's a brain-bender.

questions.question: Which South African province was formerly known as the Transvaal?
options: Gauteng, Limpopo, Mpumalanga, and North West
questions.explanation: The old Transvaal province was split into FOUR provinces in 1994: Gauteng, Limpopo (formerly Northern Province), Mpumalanga (formerly Eastern Transvaal), and North West. SA went from 4 provinces to 9 after democracy. That's a geography deep cut!

questions.question: What is the Karoo?
options: A vast semi-desert region in the interior of South Africa
A type of traditional SA music
questions.explanation: The Karoo is a vast semi-desert that covers nearly a third of South Africa! It's divided into the Great Karoo and Little Karoo. Known for wide open spaces, lamb, fossils, and stargazing (some of the clearest skies on Earth). The name comes from a Khoisan word meaning 'land of thirst.'

questions.question: Three wise men from Limpopo are given hats from a bag of 3 red and 2 blue hats. Each can see the other two's hats but not their own. They're asked in order. Man 1 (sees the other two) says 'I don't know my hat colour.' Man 2 (hearing Man 1, seeing Man 3) says 'I don't know either.' What colour is Man 3's hat?
questions.explanation: If Man 1 saw 2 blue hats, he'd know his was red (only 2 blue exist). He doesn't know, so he sees at most 1 blue hat. Man 2 knows this. If Man 2 saw a blue hat on Man 3, he'd reason: 'Man 1 saw at most 1 blue, and I see blue on Man 3. If I were also blue, Man 1 would've seen 2 blue and known. So I must be red.' But Man 2 doesn't know, meaning he DOESN'T see blue on Man 3. Therefore Man 3's hat is RED. Pure logical deduction — only the sharpest 1% crack this one!

questions.question: The Mapungubwe Kingdom, which predated Great Zimbabwe, was located in which present-day South African province?
questions.explanation: The Kingdom of Mapungubwe (900-1300 AD) was located at the confluence of the Limpopo and Shashe rivers in what is now Limpopo Province. It was one of the first kingdoms in southern Africa, with gold smelting and trade links to East Africa and Asia. The famous golden rhino was found there. Deep SA history that most people don't know!

questions.question: Who composed the original melody of 'Nkosi Sikelel' iAfrika', part of the South African national anthem?
questions.explanation: Enoch Sontonga, a Methodist school teacher from the Eastern Cape, composed 'Nkosi Sikelel' iAfrika' in 1897. It was originally a hymn sung at his school. It became the ANC anthem and later part of the national anthem after 1994. The SA anthem is unique — it uses five of the country's eleven languages in a single song!

questions.question: You're on a game show in Mzansi. Behind 3 doors: one has a Krugerrand, the others have nothing. You pick Door 1. The host (who knows what's behind each door) opens Door 3, showing nothing. He offers you the chance to switch to Door 2. What's the probability of winning if you switch?
questions.explanation: This is the famous Monty Hall problem! Your initial pick has a 1/3 chance of being right, meaning there's a 2/3 chance the Krugerrand is behind one of the other doors. When the host reveals an empty door, that entire 2/3 probability collapses onto the remaining unopened door. Switching gives you 2/3 odds vs staying at 1/3. Most people's intuition screams 50/50, but the maths don't lie. If you got this through pure reasoning, you're a proper 1-percenter. Lekker!
